preamble: The transformation from a voiced palatal plosive /ɟ/ to a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ represents a complex phonetic shift involving both lenition and a change in the place of articulation. While palatalization (velar to palatal) is a common sound change, the shift from a palatal stop to a velar fricative is less frequent and typically occurs within specific phonological environments.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (Plosive to Fricative), Depalatalization/Velarization (Palatal to Velar)

examples.note: In leniting environments, slender dental or velar stops, which are prone to converge phonetically to a voiced palatal plosive /ɟ/, can subsequently undergo lenition to a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/. This occurs as part of a broader process where plosives become fricatives.
